chapters.title: Preparedness Foundations
chapters.summary: Core household readiness principles that stay usable offline and under stress.

sections.heading: Start With The Risks You Actually Face
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Preparedness works better when you plan against likely local problems first.

- List the disruptions your household is most likely to face.
- Prioritize water, light, communication, warmth, and essential medications before edge-case gear.
- Write down the first three actions for each likely disruption so they are still clear when everyone is tired or stressed.

Talk to neighbors and check your county or city emergency management website for hazard maps specific to your area. Earthquake fault lines, flood zones, wildfire interfaces, and severe storm corridors all shift the priority list. If you live near a river, water contamination and road closures may matter more than wind damage. If you rent an apartment, your plan will look different from a homeowner with a garage full of storage space. The point is to match your preparation to the disruptions that are actually likely where you live, not to a generic list written for somewhere else.
sections.plainText: Preparedness works better when you plan against likely local problems first. List the disruptions your household is most likely to face. Prioritize water, light, communication, warmth, and essential medications before edge-case gear. Write down the first three actions for each likely disruption so they are still clear when everyone is tired or stressed. Talk to neighbors and check your county or city emergency management website for hazard maps specific to your area. Earthquake fault lines, flood zones, wildfire interfaces, and severe storm corridors all shift the priority list. If you live near a river, water contamination and road closures may matter more than wind damage. If you rent an apartment, your plan will look different from a homeowner with a garage full of storage space. The point is to match your preparation to the disruptions that are actually likely where you live, not to a generic list written for somewhere else.

sections.heading: Build Layers Instead Of Single-Point Fixes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
A resilient plan has backups for the basics.

- Keep more than one light source and more than one charging option.
- Spread critical supplies across the home so one failure or one missed refill does not wipe out the whole plan.
- Review the plan on a fixed schedule instead of waiting for an emergency to reveal the gaps.

Think of your plan as a set of overlapping safety nets. If your primary flashlight breaks, a backup headlamp keeps you moving. If the power bank is dead, a car charger or hand-crank radio still works. Store water in at least two separate locations so a single leak does not drain your entire supply. The same logic applies to information: keep critical phone numbers on paper, in your phone, and with a trusted neighbor. Redundancy feels wasteful in calm times, but during an actual disruption it is the difference between a manageable inconvenience and a genuine crisis.
sections.plainText: A resilient plan has backups for the basics. Keep more than one light source and more than one charging option. Spread critical supplies across the home so one failure or one missed refill does not wipe out the whole plan. Review the plan on a fixed schedule instead of waiting for an emergency to reveal the gaps. Think of your plan as a set of overlapping safety nets. If your primary flashlight breaks, a backup headlamp keeps you moving. If the power bank is dead, a car charger or hand-crank radio still works. Store water in at least two separate locations so a single leak does not drain your entire supply. The same logic applies to information: keep critical phone numbers on paper, in your phone, and with a trusted neighbor. Redundancy feels wasteful in calm times, but during an actual disruption it is the difference between a manageable inconvenience and a genuine crisis.

sections.heading: Keep A One-Page Household Reference
sections.bodyMarkdown:
In a stressful hour, the best plan is often the shortest one you can still read quickly.

- Keep one page with household contacts, meeting points, medication reminders, and the location of your most important supplies.
- Store printed copies in a kitchen drawer, a go-bag, and the glove box.
- Use the same names for rooms, bins, and kits that your household actually uses so nobody has to translate under stress.
- Update the page whenever a phone number, address, or medication changes.

Include the address and cross streets of your home on the reference sheet since stressed family members or visitors may not remember them when calling for help. Add your utility shutoff locations with a brief note on how to operate each valve or breaker. If anyone in the household takes daily medication, list the drug name, dosage, prescribing doctor, and pharmacy phone number. Laminate the sheet or place it in a resealable plastic bag so it survives water exposure. Review and reprint the page at least twice a year or whenever a key detail changes.
sections.plainText: In a stressful hour, the best plan is often the shortest one you can still read quickly. Keep one page with household contacts, meeting points, medication reminders, and the location of your most important supplies. Store printed copies in a kitchen drawer, a go-bag, and the glove box. Use the same names for rooms, bins, and kits that your household actually uses so nobody has to translate under stress. Update the page whenever a phone number, address, or medication changes. Include the address and cross streets of your home on the reference sheet since stressed family members or visitors may not remember them when calling for help. Add your utility shutoff locations with a brief note on how to operate each valve or breaker. If anyone in the household takes daily medication, list the drug name, dosage, prescribing doctor, and pharmacy phone number. Laminate the sheet or place it in a resealable plastic bag so it survives water exposure. Review and reprint the page at least twice a year or whenever a key detail changes.

sections.heading: Use Short Review Cycles Instead Of Big Overhauls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Preparedness stays usable when maintenance is small, predictable, and easy to repeat.

- Pick one short review window each month to check water dates, batteries, flashlights, medications, and kit locations.
- Replace used items immediately instead of waiting for a full yearly reset.
- Walk the household through one simple scenario at a time, such as a power outage or evacuation pickup.
- Write down any confusion you notice and turn it into a checklist, label, or note before the next review.

Attach your review to a habit you already have, such as the first Saturday of each month or the day you pay rent. Spending ten minutes checking supplies on a regular schedule prevents the two-hour scramble when a storm watch appears in the forecast. Keep a simple tally of what you checked and what you replaced so you can see patterns over time. If you find the same item expired or missing twice in a row, move it to a more visible spot or add it to a recurring shopping list. Small consistent effort compounds into genuine readiness.
sections.plainText: Preparedness stays usable when maintenance is small, predictable, and easy to repeat. Pick one short review window each month to check water dates, batteries, flashlights, medications, and kit locations. Replace used items immediately instead of waiting for a full yearly reset. Walk the household through one simple scenario at a time, such as a power outage or evacuation pickup. Write down any confusion you notice and turn it into a checklist, label, or note before the next review. chapters.title: Family Plan And Emergency Contacts
chapters.summary: Household communication plans, meeting points, and emergency contact organization.

sections.heading: Build A Household Communication Plan
sections.bodyMarkdown:
A plan everyone knows is better than a perfect plan nobody remembers.

- Agree on one primary contact method. Texting is more reliable than calling during high-traffic events.
- Choose one out-of-area contact person everyone can check in with.
- Write the plan on a card each person carries, not just in a phone.
- Practice the plan at least once so everyone knows the steps when they are stressed.

Agree on a primary and backup method of communication before a disruption happens. Text messages often go through when voice calls cannot because they require less network bandwidth. Choose an out-of-area contact that all household members can reach independently since local phone networks may be overloaded while long-distance connections still work. Practice the plan at least once so everyone knows the steps without needing to look them up. If young children are part of the household, make sure they can recite or find the emergency contact number without help from a phone screen.
sections.plainText: A plan everyone knows is better than a perfect plan nobody remembers. Agree on one primary contact method. Texting is more reliable than calling during high-traffic events. Choose one out-of-area contact person everyone can check in with. Write the plan on a card each person carries, not just in a phone. Practice the plan at least once so everyone knows the steps when they are stressed. Agree on a primary and backup method of communication before a disruption happens. Text messages often go through when voice calls cannot because they require less network bandwidth. Choose an out-of-area contact that all household members can reach independently since local phone networks may be overloaded while long-distance connections still work. Practice the plan at least once so everyone knows the steps without needing to look them up. If young children are part of the household, make sure they can recite or find the emergency contact number without help from a phone screen.

sections.heading: Choose Meeting Points And Routes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Pick two meeting points and make sure everyone can find them without a phone.

- **Primary:** A visible spot near your home, such as the mailbox or a neighbor's driveway.
- **Secondary:** A location outside your neighborhood in case the area is evacuated, such as a community center or school.
- Walk or drive both routes at least once.
- If children are in school or daycare, confirm the facility's reunification plan.

Select one meeting point within walking distance of your home and a second one outside your immediate neighborhood in case the area is evacuated. Identify at least two driving routes to each point since road closures, downed trees, or flooding can block the most obvious path. Walk or drive each route at least once so the directions feel familiar under stress. If household members attend different schools or workplaces during the day, make sure each person knows which meeting point to head for depending on the time of day. Post the meeting point addresses and a simple map near the main exit of your home.
sections.plainText: Pick two meeting points and make sure everyone can find them without a phone. Primary: A visible spot near your home, such as the mailbox or a neighbor's driveway. Secondary: A location outside your neighborhood in case the area is evacuated, such as a community center or school. Walk or drive both routes at least once. If children are in school or daycare, confirm the facility's reunification plan. Select one meeting point within walking distance of your home and a second one outside your immediate neighborhood in case the area is evacuated. Post the meeting point addresses and a simple map near the main exit of your home.

sections.heading: Prepare Emergency Contact Lists
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Keep a written contact list that works when phones are dead or lost.

- Include household members, the out-of-area contact, neighbors, doctors, insurance, and your local utility companies.
- Print or write copies for each go-bag, the car, and one posted at home.
- Update the list every six months or after any change in phone numbers or addresses.
- Store a digital copy in a notes app that works offline.

Organize your contacts into three groups: immediate household members, local emergency services and utilities, and out-of-area backup contacts. Include the non-emergency police line, your power company's outage reporting number, and your water utility. For medical contacts, list your primary care doctor, nearest urgent care, and pharmacy with hours. Store the list digitally in this app, print a hard copy for your kitchen and go-bag, and share it with at least one trusted neighbor. Update the list any time a phone number, address, or provider changes.
sections.plainText: Keep a written contact list that works when phones are dead or lost. Include household members, the out-of-area contact, neighbors, doctors, insurance, and your local utility companies. Print or write copies for each go-bag, the car, and one posted at home. Update the list every six months or after any change in phone numbers or addresses. Store a digital copy in a notes app that works offline. chapters.title: Water
chapters.summary: Safe water storage, rotation, and treatment for household preparedness.

sections.heading: Store Drinking Water Safely
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Water is the first supply to run short and the hardest to improvise safely.

- Store at least one gallon per person per day for a minimum of three days.
- Use food-grade containers with tight-fitting lids.
- Keep containers in a cool, dark place away from chemicals and direct sunlight.
- Label each container with the fill date.
- Do not reuse containers that held milk, juice, or other non-water liquids.

If you have infants, nursing mothers, or anyone who is ill, plan for additional water beyond the one-gallon-per-person baseline. Pets need roughly one ounce of water per pound of body weight per day. In hot climates or during physical activity, double your daily estimate. Two-liter soda bottles made of PETE plastic work well for storage after thorough cleaning and rinsing, but avoid containers that previously held milk or juice since residual sugars encourage bacterial growth. Consider storing a few cases of commercially bottled water as a grab-and-go backup alongside your larger containers.
sections.plainText: Water is the first supply to run short and the hardest to improvise safely. Store at least one gallon per person per day for a minimum of three days. Use food-grade containers with tight-fitting lids. Keep containers in a cool, dark place away from chemicals and direct sunlight. Label each container with the fill date. Do not reuse containers that held milk, juice, or other non-water liquids. If you have infants, nursing mothers, or anyone who is ill, plan for additional water beyond the one-gallon-per-person baseline. Pets need roughly one ounce of water per pound of body weight per day. In hot climates or during physical activity, double your daily estimate. Two-liter soda bottles made of PETE plastic work well for storage after thorough cleaning and rinsing, but avoid containers that previously held milk or juice since residual sugars encourage bacterial growth. Consider storing a few cases of commercially bottled water as a grab-and-go backup alongside your larger containers.

sections.heading: Rotate And Test Stored Water
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Stored water stays safe for a long time if the containers are sealed and clean, but rotation keeps you confident in your supply.

- Replace stored water every six months, or sooner if containers show cloudiness, odor, or damage.
- Move the oldest containers to the front of storage so they get used first.
- Inspect seals and lids during each rotation.
- Write the next rotation date where you can see it without moving containers.

Mark each container clearly with the fill date using a permanent marker. When you rotate, pour the old water into houseplants or use it for cleaning rather than wasting it. If you notice an off taste but no cloudiness or odor, the water is likely safe but may have absorbed flavors from the container. Commercially sealed bottles with intact seals can last well beyond six months, but home-filled containers should follow the six-month rotation cycle. Keep a simple rotation log in the Notes section of this app so you always know when the next swap is due.
sections.plainText: Stored water stays safe for a long time if the containers are sealed and clean, but rotation keeps you confident in your supply. Replace stored water every six months, or sooner if containers show cloudiness, odor, or damage. Move the oldest containers to the front of storage so they get used first. Inspect seals and lids during each rotation. Write the next rotation date where you can see it without moving containers. Mark each container clearly with the fill date using a permanent marker. When you rotate, pour the old water into houseplants or use it for cleaning rather than wasting it. If you notice an off taste but no cloudiness or odor, the water is likely safe but may have absorbed flavors from the container. Commercially sealed bottles with intact seals can last well beyond six months, but home-filled containers should follow the six-month rotation cycle. Keep a simple rotation log in the Notes section of this app so you always know when the next swap is due.

sections.heading: Treat Water When Supply Is Uncertain
sections.bodyMarkdown:
If your stored supply runs out or tap water safety is in question, treat water before drinking.

- **Boiling:** Bring water to a full rolling boil for at least one minute. Let it cool before storing.
- **Disinfection:** If you cannot boil, use unscented household chlorine bleach (6% sodium hypochlorite). Add 8 drops per gallon, stir, and wait 30 minutes. The water should have a slight chlorine smell.
- Do not use water from radiators, hot water boilers, swimming pools, or water beds for drinking.
- When in doubt about safety, use bottled water or wait for an official all-clear.

If you cannot boil water and do not have bleach, household iodine (2% tincture) can also disinfect: add five drops per quart of clear water and wait 30 minutes. Portable water filters rated for bacteria and protozoa are a worthwhile addition to your kit, but most consumer filters do not remove viruses, so chemical treatment remains the safest standalone option. Avoid collecting water from sources near industrial sites, agricultural runoff, or areas with visible contamination. When in doubt, layer your treatment: filter first to remove sediment, then disinfect with boiling or chemicals.
sections.plainText: If your stored supply runs out or tap water safety is in question, treat water before drinking. Boiling: Bring water to a full rolling boil for at least one minute. Let it cool before storing. Disinfection: If you cannot boil, use unscented household chlorine bleach (6% sodium hypochlorite). Add 8 drops per gallon, stir, and wait 30 minutes. The water should have a slight chlorine smell. Do not use water from radiators, hot water boilers, swimming pools, or water beds for drinking. When in doubt about safety, use bottled water or wait for an official all-clear. If you cannot boil water and do not have bleach, household iodine (2% tincture) can also disinfect: add five drops per quart of clear water and wait 30 minutes. Portable water filters rated for bacteria and protozoa are a worthwhile addition to your kit, but most consumer filters do not remove viruses, so chemical treatment remains the safest standalone option. Avoid collecting water from sources near industrial sites, agricultural runoff, or areas with visible contamination. When in doubt, layer your treatment: filter first to remove sediment, then disinfect with boiling or chemicals.

chapters.title: Food
chapters.summary: Shelf-stable food planning, rotation, and safe handling during outages.

sections.heading: Build A Shelf-Stable Food Supply
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Stock food your household will actually eat, not just whatever has a long shelf life.

- Keep at least a three-day supply of non-perishable food per person.
- Choose items that require no refrigeration, little or no water to prepare, and no cooking if possible.
- Include canned proteins, nut butters, dried fruit, crackers, granola bars, and ready-to-eat cereals.
- Keep a manual can opener with the food supply.
- Account for dietary restrictions, allergies, infant formula, and pet food.

Focus on foods your household actually eats. A pantry full of unfamiliar items will go untouched during stress. Canned soups, beans, tuna, peanut butter, crackers, dried fruit, and granola bars all store well and require little or no preparation. Check sodium content if anyone in the household has dietary restrictions. Aim for a mix of protein, carbohydrates, and fats to maintain energy. Store food in a cool, dark, dry area and keep it off the floor to avoid moisture and pests. A shelf-stable supply of three to seven days covers most short-duration disruptions.
sections.plainText: Stock food your household will actually eat, not just whatever has a long shelf life. Keep at least a three-day supply of non-perishable food per person. Choose items that require no refrigeration, little or no water to prepare, and no cooking if possible. Include canned proteins, nut butters, dried fruit, crackers, granola bars, and ready-to-eat cereals. Keep a manual can opener with the food supply. Account for dietary restrictions, allergies, infant formula, and pet food. Focus on foods your household actually eats. A pantry full of unfamiliar items will go untouched during stress. Canned soups, beans, tuna, peanut butter, crackers, dried fruit, and granola bars all store well and require little or no preparation. Store food in a cool, dark, dry area and keep it off the floor to avoid moisture and pests. A shelf-stable supply of three to seven days covers most short-duration disruptions.

sections.heading: Rotate And Track Stored Food
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Food you forget about is food you cannot trust.

- Check expiration dates every three months.
- Move older items to the front and use them in regular meals before they expire.
- Replace consumed or expired items promptly.
- Keep an inventory list near the storage area. The Inventory tab in this app can help track quantities and dates.

Use the first-in, first-out method: place newer items at the back and move older items to the front. Check expiration dates quarterly and incorporate items approaching their dates into regular meals. Canned goods are often safe well beyond their best-by dates if the cans are not dented, swollen, or rusted, but rotating them keeps quality high. Track your stored food inventory in this app to avoid duplicate purchases and ensure a balanced supply. If you donate near-expiry items to a food bank, replace them immediately so your emergency stock stays complete.
sections.plainText: Food you forget about is food you cannot trust. Check expiration dates every three months. Move older items to the front and use them in regular meals before they expire. Replace consumed or expired items promptly. Keep an inventory list near the storage area. The Inventory tab in this app can help track quantities and dates. sections.heading: Manage Refrigerated Food During Outages
sections.bodyMarkdown:
The goal is to keep cold food safe as long as possible without opening the door unnecessarily.

- A closed refrigerator keeps food safe for about four hours.
- A full freezer holds temperature for roughly 48 hours; a half-full freezer for about 24 hours.
- Resist the urge to check. Each door opening shortens the safe window.
- If power has been out more than four hours, discard perishable items that have risen above 40°F (4°C).
- When in doubt, throw it out. Foodborne illness is a bigger risk than wasted groceries.

A full freezer holds its temperature for roughly 48 hours if the door stays closed. A half-full freezer holds for about 24 hours. Group frozen items tightly together to help them stay cold longer. If you know a storm is coming, fill zip-lock bags with water and freeze them in advance to add thermal mass. Use a refrigerator thermometer so you can check the internal temperature without opening the door. Once the refrigerator rises above 40 degrees Fahrenheit (4 degrees Celsius) for more than two hours, perishable items like dairy, meat, and cut produce should be discarded.
sections.plainText: The goal is to keep cold food safe as long as possible without opening the door unnecessarily. A closed refrigerator keeps food safe for about four hours. A full freezer holds temperature for roughly 48 hours; a half-full freezer for about 24 hours. Resist the urge to check. Each door opening shortens the safe window. If power has been out more than four hours, discard perishable items that have risen above 40 degrees F (4 degrees C). When in doubt, throw it out. Foodborne illness is a bigger risk than wasted groceries. A full freezer holds its temperature for roughly 48 hours if the door stays closed. A half-full freezer holds for about 24 hours. Group frozen items tightly together to help them stay cold longer. If you know a storm is coming, fill zip-lock bags with water and freeze them in advance to add thermal mass. Use a refrigerator thermometer so you can check the internal temperature without opening the door. chapters.title: Power Outage
chapters.summary: Preparation, first-hour response, and device management during power outages.

sections.heading: Prepare Before An Outage
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Preparation before the lights go out is faster and cheaper than improvising afterward.

- Charge all phones, tablets, and battery banks when a storm or outage is expected.
- Set refrigerator and freezer to their coldest settings to extend the safe window.
- Fill bathtubs or large containers with water in case water pumps lose power.
- Place a flashlight or lantern in each occupied room.
- Unplug sensitive electronics to prevent surge damage when power returns.

If a storm or planned outage is forecast, charge all phones, tablets, and portable battery packs to full. Fill bathtubs and large pots with water for flushing toilets and washing. Set your refrigerator and freezer to the coldest settings in advance so they start from a lower temperature. Gather flashlights, batteries, and a battery-powered or hand-crank radio in one easily accessible spot. Know where your circuit breaker panel is and how to turn off individual circuits. Unplug sensitive electronics like computers and televisions to protect them from power surges when service is restored.
sections.plainText: Preparation before the lights go out is faster and cheaper than improvising afterward. Charge all phones, tablets, and battery banks when a storm or outage is expected. Set refrigerator and freezer to their coldest settings to extend the safe window. Fill bathtubs or large containers with water in case water pumps lose power. Place a flashlight or lantern in each occupied room. Unplug sensitive electronics to prevent surge damage when power returns. If a storm or planned outage is forecast, charge all phones, tablets, and portable battery packs to full. Fill bathtubs and large pots with water for flushing toilets and washing. Unplug sensitive electronics like computers and televisions to protect them from power surges when service is restored.

sections.heading: Manage The First Hours Without Power
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Stay calm, account for everyone, and stabilize the basics.

- Confirm everyone in the household is safe. Check on anyone who relies on powered medical equipment.
- Switch to battery-powered lighting. Avoid candles in routine indoor use.
- Keep the refrigerator and freezer doors closed.
- Check on neighbors, especially elderly or medically vulnerable people.
- Monitor a battery-powered or hand-crank NOAA weather radio for updates.
- Report the outage to your utility if it has not been acknowledged.

Stay calm and take stock of what you have before making decisions. Check on elderly neighbors or anyone in your household who depends on powered medical equipment. If you have a generator, run it outdoors only and at least 20 feet from any window, door, or vent to prevent carbon monoxide poisoning. Use flashlights or battery lanterns instead of candles whenever possible to reduce fire risk. Keep one phone turned off and in reserve so you have a working device later if the outage extends. Listen to a battery-powered radio for utility updates rather than relying on internet connectivity.
sections.plainText: Stay calm, account for everyone, and stabilize the basics. Confirm everyone in the household is safe. Check on anyone who relies on powered medical equipment. Switch to battery-powered lighting. Avoid candles in routine indoor use. Keep the refrigerator and freezer doors closed. Check on neighbors, especially elderly or medically vulnerable people. Monitor a battery-powered or hand-crank NOAA weather radio for updates. Report the outage to your utility if it has not been acknowledged. Stay calm and take stock of what you have before making decisions. Check on elderly neighbors or anyone in your household who depends on powered medical equipment. If you have a generator, run it outdoors only and at least 20 feet from any window, door, or vent to prevent carbon monoxide poisoning. Use flashlights or battery lanterns instead of candles whenever possible to reduce fire risk. Keep one phone turned off and in reserve so you have a working device later if the outage extends. Listen to a battery-powered radio for utility updates rather than relying on internet connectivity.

sections.heading: Charge Devices And Conserve Battery
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Phones are your most important communication tool. Treat battery life as a critical resource.

- Switch phones to low-power mode immediately.
- Turn off Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and background app refresh if not needed.
- Reduce screen brightness to the minimum usable level.
- Charge from your largest battery bank first; save smaller portable chargers for later.
- A car charger works if the car is running, but never run a vehicle in a closed garage.

Switch phones to low-power mode immediately and reduce screen brightness to the minimum usable level. Disable background app refresh, location services, and push notifications for non-essential apps. A car charger can replenish a phone battery in roughly 30 minutes if you run the engine. Portable battery packs in the 10,000 to 20,000 milliamp-hour range can recharge a phone three to six times. A hand-crank or solar charger is slower but provides power indefinitely without fuel. Prioritize charging the device you will use for emergency communication and keep secondary devices powered off until needed.
sections.plainText: Phones are your most important communication tool. Treat battery life as a critical resource. Switch phones to low-power mode immediately. Turn off Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and background app refresh if not needed. Reduce screen brightness to the minimum usable level. Charge from your largest battery bank first; save smaller portable chargers for later. A car charger works if the car is running, but never run a vehicle in a closed garage. A car charger can replenish a phone battery in roughly 30 minutes if you run the engine. Portable battery packs in the 10,000 to 20,000 milliamp-hour range can recharge a phone three to six times. A hand-crank or solar charger is slower but provides power indefinitely without fuel. Prioritize charging the device you will use for emergency communication and keep secondary devices powered off until needed.

chapters.title: Cooking Without Power
chapters.summary: Safe outdoor cooking options and indoor limitations during power outages.

sections.heading: Safe Outdoor Cooking Options
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Outdoor cooking is the safest option when the power is out.

- Use a propane or charcoal grill outdoors only, at least 10 feet from structures.
- A portable camp stove with proper ventilation works well for simple meals.
- Never use charcoal or gas grills inside the house, garage, or any enclosed space.
- Keep fuel stored away from heat sources and out of direct sunlight.
- Have a fire extinguisher nearby when cooking with open flame.

Propane camp stoves are the most common outdoor cooking option and are easy to control. Keep at least two spare fuel canisters and store them upright in a cool, shaded area away from the house. Charcoal grills work well but take longer to heat and require proper ventilation. If using a fire pit, clear a ten-foot radius of dry leaves and debris and keep a bucket of water or a garden hose nearby. Never use any fuel-burning stove, grill, or fire pit inside the home, inside a garage, or under a carport, even with the door open. Carbon monoxide is odorless and can accumulate quickly in enclosed spaces.
sections.plainText: Outdoor cooking is the safest option when the power is out. Use a propane or charcoal grill outdoors only, at least 10 feet from structures. A portable camp stove with proper ventilation works well for simple meals. Never use charcoal or gas grills inside the house, garage, or any enclosed space. Keep fuel stored away from heat sources and out of direct sunlight. Have a fire extinguisher nearby when cooking with open flame. Propane camp stoves are the most common outdoor cooking option and are easy to control. Keep at least two spare fuel canisters and store them upright in a cool, shaded area away from the house. Charcoal grills work well but take longer to heat and require proper ventilation. If using a fire pit, clear a ten-foot radius of dry leaves and debris and keep a bucket of water or a garden hose nearby. Never use any fuel-burning stove, grill, or fire pit inside the home, inside a garage, or under a carport, even with the door open. Carbon monoxide is odorless and can accumulate quickly in enclosed spaces.

sections.heading: Indoor Cooking Limitations And Risks
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Indoor cooking without proper ventilation creates serious carbon monoxide and fire risks.

- Do not use outdoor grills, camp stoves, or generators inside any enclosed space.
- Do not heat your home with a gas oven or stovetop.
- If you have a gas stove that still functions during a power outage, use it briefly for simple heating tasks with windows cracked for ventilation. Do not leave it unattended.
- Carbon monoxide is odorless and deadly. If your CO detector alarms, leave the building immediately and call 911.
- Prefer no-cook food options indoors when the power is out.

The only indoor cooking devices considered safe during a power outage are those specifically designed for indoor use, such as a fondue pot with approved fuel or a chafing dish with canned heat like Sterno. Even these should be used in a well-ventilated room with a window cracked open. Gas stoves can technically produce a meal, but using them for extended periods without powered ventilation increases carbon monoxide risk. If you must use a gas stove briefly, open a window in the kitchen and do not use the oven for heating the house. Install a battery-powered carbon monoxide detector in the kitchen if you do not already have one.
sections.plainText: Indoor cooking without proper ventilation creates serious carbon monoxide and fire risks. Do not use outdoor grills, camp stoves, or generators inside any enclosed space. Do not heat your home with a gas oven or stovetop. If you have a gas stove that still functions during a power outage, use it briefly for simple heating tasks with windows cracked for ventilation. Do not leave it unattended. Carbon monoxide is odorless and deadly. If your CO detector alarms, leave the building immediately and call 911. Prefer no-cook food options indoors when the power is out. The only indoor cooking devices considered safe during a power outage are those specifically designed for indoor use, such as a fondue pot with approved fuel or a chafing dish with canned heat like Sterno. Even these should be used in a well-ventilated room with a window cracked open. Gas stoves can technically produce a meal, but using them for extended periods without powered ventilation increases carbon monoxide risk. If you must use a gas stove briefly, open a window in the kitchen and do not use the oven for heating the house. Install a battery-powered carbon monoxide detector in the kitchen if you do not already have one.

sections.heading: Plan No-Cook And Low-Water Meals First
sections.bodyMarkdown:
When power is limited, the safest meal is often the one that needs the least heat, fuel, and cleanup.

- Keep shelf-stable foods that can be eaten cold or warmed briefly, such as canned soups, tuna, nut butter, crackers, and ready-to-eat cereal.
- Use refrigerated food early while it is still safely cold, and move to pantry food as the outage continues.
- Avoid meals that require long simmer times or large amounts of dishwashing water.
- Keep a manual can opener, simple utensils, and trash bags with your emergency food supplies.

When power and water are limited, meals that require no cooking and minimal cleanup save both resources and energy. Peanut butter on crackers, canned fruit, granola bars, trail mix, and beef jerky all provide calories without heat or water. If you need a warm meal for morale, heat water for instant oatmeal or soup using a camp stove rather than cooking a full dish. Paper plates and disposable utensils reduce the need for wash water. Plan your meals in order of perishability: eat refrigerator items first, then freezer items, then shelf-stable supplies.
sections.plainText: When power is limited, the safest meal is often the one that needs the least heat, fuel, and cleanup. Keep shelf-stable foods that can be eaten cold or warmed briefly, such as canned soups, tuna, nut butter, crackers, and ready-to-eat cereal. Use refrigerated food early while it is still safely cold, and move to pantry food as the outage continues. Avoid meals that require long simmer times or large amounts of dishwashing water. Keep a manual can opener, simple utensils, and trash bags with your emergency food supplies. When power and water are limited, meals that require no cooking and minimal cleanup save both resources and energy. Peanut butter on crackers, canned fruit, granola bars, trail mix, and beef jerky all provide calories without heat or water. If you need a warm meal for morale, heat water for instant oatmeal or soup using a camp stove rather than cooking a full dish. Paper plates and disposable utensils reduce the need for wash water. Plan your meals in order of perishability: eat refrigerator items first, then freezer items, then shelf-stable supplies.

sections.heading: Handle Fuel, Fire, And Ventilation Deliberately
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Fuel mistakes during an outage create fast-moving fire and carbon monoxide hazards.

- Store spare propane, butane, charcoal, and liquid fuels outside living areas and away from ignition sources.
- Let stoves, grills, and lanterns cool fully before refueling or moving them.
- Keep a working smoke alarm and carbon monoxide alarm in the home before you rely on any combustion appliance.
- If conditions are windy, wet, or crowded, choose no-cook meals instead of forcing a flame-based setup.

Keep a running inventory of how much fuel you have and estimate how many meals each canister or bag of charcoal can produce. A standard 16-ounce propane canister fuels roughly one to two hours of cooking on a single-burner camp stove. Charcoal briquettes need about 30 minutes to reach cooking temperature and a 10-pound bag covers three to four grilling sessions. Store all fuel away from heat sources and out of direct sunlight. After cooking, let all equipment cool completely before moving it or storing it. Dispose of charcoal ash in a metal container with a lid, not in a plastic trash bag.
sections.plainText: Fuel mistakes during an outage create fast-moving fire and carbon monoxide hazards. Store spare propane, butane, charcoal, and liquid fuels outside living areas and away from ignition sources. Let stoves, grills, and lanterns cool fully before refueling or moving them. Keep a working smoke alarm and carbon monoxide alarm in the home before you rely on any combustion appliance. If conditions are windy, wet, or crowded, choose no-cook meals instead of forcing a flame-based setup. Keep a running inventory of how much fuel you have and estimate how many meals each canister or bag of charcoal can produce. A standard 16-ounce propane canister fuels roughly one to two hours of cooking on a single-burner camp stove. Charcoal briquettes need about 30 minutes to reach cooking temperature and a 10-pound bag covers three to four grilling sessions. Store all fuel away from heat sources and out of direct sunlight. After cooking, let all equipment cool completely before moving it or storing it. Dispose of charcoal ash in a metal container with a lid, not in a plastic trash bag.

chapters.title: Warmth And Shelter
chapters.summary: Staying warm at home during cold-weather power outages.

sections.heading: Keep Your Home Warm Without Power
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Retaining heat is easier than generating it.

- Close off rooms you are not using and gather in one interior room.
- Hang blankets over windows and doorways to slow heat loss.
- If you have a fireplace or wood stove, use it according to the manufacturer's instructions. Keep the damper open while burning.
- Do not use gas ovens, grills, or portable generators for indoor heating.
- Let faucets drip slightly to prevent pipe freezing during extended cold outages.

Close off rooms you are not using and hang blankets over doorways to concentrate heat in a smaller area. If you have a fireplace or wood stove, make sure the chimney is clean and the flue is open before lighting a fire. Keep a supply of dry firewood or manufactured fire logs indoors so you do not have to go outside repeatedly. Close curtains at night to retain heat and open south-facing curtains during the day to capture solar warmth. Never use a gas oven, charcoal grill, or portable propane heater indoors as a heat source. These produce carbon monoxide and can be fatal in an enclosed space.
sections.plainText: Retaining heat is easier than generating it. Close off rooms you are not using and gather in one interior room. Hang blankets over windows and doorways to slow heat loss. If you have a fireplace or wood stove, use it according to the manufacturer's instructions. Keep the damper open while burning. Do not use gas ovens, grills, or portable generators for indoor heating. Let faucets drip slightly to prevent pipe freezing during extended cold outages. Close off rooms you are not using and hang blankets over doorways to concentrate heat in a smaller area. If you have a fireplace or wood stove, make sure the chimney is clean and the flue is open before lighting a fire. Keep a supply of dry firewood or manufactured fire logs indoors so you do not have to go outside repeatedly. Close curtains at night to retain heat and open south-facing curtains during the day to capture solar warmth. Never use a gas oven, charcoal grill, or portable propane heater indoors as a heat source. These produce carbon monoxide and can be fatal in an enclosed space.

sections.heading: Dress For Cold Indoors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Layering is more effective than one heavy garment.

- Wear a base layer of moisture-wicking fabric against your skin.
- Add an insulating middle layer such as fleece or wool.
- Top with a windproof or water-resistant outer layer if moving outside.
- Wear a warm hat indoors. A large percentage of body heat escapes through the head.
- Keep feet warm with wool socks and insulated slippers.
- Sleeping bags rated for cold weather are more effective than stacked blankets.

Layer clothing rather than relying on a single heavy garment. A base layer of moisture-wicking fabric, a middle insulating layer of fleece or wool, and an outer wind-blocking layer work together more effectively than one thick coat. Wear a warm hat indoors since a significant amount of body heat escapes through the head. Thick socks and slippers keep feet warm on cold floors. Keep a set of dry, warm clothing in your bedroom so you can dress quickly if the temperature drops overnight. If anyone in the household is elderly, very young, or has a circulatory condition, monitor them more closely since they lose body heat faster.
sections.plainText: Layering is more effective than one heavy garment. Wear a base layer of moisture-wicking fabric against your skin. Add an insulating middle layer such as fleece or wool. Top with a windproof or water-resistant outer layer if moving outside. Wear a warm hat indoors. A large percentage of body heat escapes through the head. Keep feet warm with wool socks and insulated slippers. Sleeping bags rated for cold weather are more effective than stacked blankets. Layer clothing rather than relying on a single heavy garment. A base layer of moisture-wicking fabric, a middle insulating layer of fleece or wool, and an outer wind-blocking layer work together more effectively than one thick coat. Wear a warm hat indoors since a significant amount of body heat escapes through the head. Thick socks and slippers keep feet warm on cold floors. Keep a set of dry, warm clothing in your bedroom so you can dress quickly if the temperature drops overnight. If anyone in the household is elderly, very young, or has a circulatory condition, monitor them more closely since they lose body heat faster.

sections.heading: Choose And Prepare A Warm Room
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Concentrating people and heat in one room makes a big difference.

- Pick a small interior room with the fewest exterior walls and windows.
- Close doors and vents to unused rooms.
- Gather blankets, sleeping bags, pillows, flashlights, water, and snacks in the room.
- If possible, choose a room with a south-facing window to gain daytime solar warmth.
- Keep pets in the room with you for shared warmth and their safety.

Pick the smallest interior room with the fewest exterior walls and windows. A room on a south-facing side of the house will be naturally warmer during the day. Move sleeping bags, blankets, and pillows into this room so the household can sleep together and share body heat. If you have a tent, setting it up inside the warm room creates an additional insulating layer that traps body heat remarkably well. Keep water, snacks, a flashlight, and a phone charger in the room so no one needs to leave frequently. Block drafts under doors with rolled towels.
sections.plainText: Concentrating people and heat in one room makes a big difference. Pick a small interior room with the fewest exterior walls and windows. Close doors and vents to unused rooms. Gather blankets, sleeping bags, pillows, flashlights, water, and snacks in the room. If possible, choose a room with a south-facing window to gain daytime solar warmth. Keep pets in the room with you for shared warmth and their safety. Pick the smallest interior room with the fewest exterior walls and windows. A room on a south-facing side of the house will be naturally warmer during the day. Move sleeping bags, blankets, and pillows into this room so the household can sleep together and share body heat. If you have a tent, setting it up inside the warm room creates an additional insulating layer that traps body heat remarkably well. Keep water, snacks, a flashlight, and a phone charger in the room so no one needs to leave frequently. Block drafts under doors with rolled towels.

chapters.title: First Aid, Hygiene, And Medications
chapters.summary: Kit organization, medication management, and hygiene without running water.

sections.heading: Organize A First Aid Kit
sections.bodyMarkdown:
A well-organized kit is faster to use when hands are shaking.

- Stock adhesive bandages, gauze pads, medical tape, antiseptic wipes, and disposable gloves.
- Include tweezers, scissors, a digital thermometer, and an instant cold pack.
- Add pain relievers, antihistamines, and anti-diarrheal medication.
- Check expiration dates every three months and replace anything used or expired.
- Keep one kit at home and a smaller version in each vehicle and go-bag.

A well-organized kit saves time when hands are shaking and light is poor. Group items by function: wound care in one pouch, medications in another, and tools like scissors and tweezers in a third. Include adhesive bandages in multiple sizes, sterile gauze pads, medical tape, an elastic bandage, antiseptic wipes, antibiotic ointment, over-the-counter pain relievers, and a digital thermometer. Add a printed first aid reference card since instructions are hard to recall under stress. Check the kit every three months for expired medications or used-up supplies, and replace items immediately after any use.
sections.plainText: A well-organized kit is faster to use when hands are shaking. Stock adhesive bandages, gauze pads, medical tape, antiseptic wipes, and disposable gloves. Include tweezers, scissors, a digital thermometer, and an instant cold pack. Add pain relievers, antihistamines, and anti-diarrheal medication. Check expiration dates every three months and replace anything used or expired. Keep one kit at home and a smaller version in each vehicle and go-bag. A well-organized kit saves time when hands are shaking and light is poor. Group items by function: wound care in one pouch, medications in another, and tools like scissors and tweezers in a third. Include adhesive bandages in multiple sizes, sterile gauze pads, medical tape, an elastic bandage, antiseptic wipes, antibiotic ointment, over-the-counter pain relievers, and a digital thermometer. Add a printed first aid reference card since instructions are hard to recall under stress. Check the kit every three months for expired medications or used-up supplies, and replace items immediately after any use.

sections.heading: Manage Medications During Disruptions
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Running out of a critical medication during a disruption is a serious risk. Plan ahead.

- Keep at least a seven-day supply of each prescription medication on hand at all times.
- Store medications in a cool, dry place. Avoid leaving them in vehicles where heat and cold extremes can degrade them.
- Maintain a written list of all medications, dosages, and prescribing doctors for each household member.
- Ask your pharmacist about emergency refill policies in your area.
- If you depend on refrigerated medication such as insulin, plan a cold-storage backup.
- **Caution:** This app does not provide dosage advice or medical diagnosis. Consult your healthcare provider for medication-specific guidance.

Keep at least a seven-day supply of all prescription medications on hand at all times. Talk to your pharmacist about getting an extra refill authorized for emergency use. Store medications in a cool, dry, dark location and check expiration dates quarterly. If anyone in the household uses insulin or another temperature-sensitive medication, have a plan for keeping it cool without electricity, such as a small insulated bag with frozen gel packs. Write down each person's medication name, dosage, prescribing doctor, and pharmacy on your household reference sheet. If you need to evacuate, medications and the reference sheet should be the first items in your go-bag.
sections.plainText: Running out of a critical medication during a disruption is a serious risk. Plan ahead. Keep at least a seven-day supply of each prescription medication on hand at all times. Store medications in a cool, dry place. Avoid leaving them in vehicles where heat and cold extremes can degrade them. Maintain a written list of all medications, dosages, and prescribing doctors for each household member. Ask your pharmacist about emergency refill policies in your area. If you depend on refrigerated medication such as insulin, plan a cold-storage backup. Caution: This app does not provide dosage advice or medical diagnosis. Consult your healthcare provider for medication-specific guidance. Keep at least a seven-day supply of all prescription medications on hand at all times. Talk to your pharmacist about getting an extra refill authorized for emergency use. Store medications in a cool, dry, dark location and check expiration dates quarterly. If anyone in the household uses insulin or another temperature-sensitive medication, have a plan for keeping it cool without electricity, such as a small insulated bag with frozen gel packs. Write down each person's medication name, dosage, prescribing doctor, and pharmacy on your household reference sheet. If you need to evacuate, medications and the reference sheet should be the first items in your go-bag.

sections.heading: Maintain Hygiene Without Running Water
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Good hygiene prevents illness when medical help may be delayed.

- Stock hand sanitizer with at least 60% alcohol for when soap and water are not available.
- Keep baby wipes or wet wipes for basic body cleaning.
- Use a bucket and small amount of water for a sponge bath if water is limited.
- Dispose of waste carefully. If toilets do not flush, use a lined bucket with a tight lid and kitty litter or sawdust to manage odor.
- Wash hands before handling food and after using the toilet, even with limited water.

Fill a five-gallon bucket with water before a disruption for flushing toilets. One bucket flush takes roughly one gallon. Hand sanitizer with at least 60 percent alcohol is effective for hand hygiene when water is unavailable, but wash with soap and water when you can since sanitizer does not remove all contaminants. Baby wipes or body wipes provide a basic cleaning option when bathing is not possible. Dispose of waste carefully: if toilets are completely non-functional, a five-gallon bucket lined with a heavy-duty garbage bag and a handful of cat litter or sawdust after each use works as a temporary latrine. Seal and dispose of bags according to local guidance.
sections.plainText: Good hygiene prevents illness when medical help may be delayed. Stock hand sanitizer with at least 60% alcohol for when soap and water are not available. Keep baby wipes or wet wipes for basic body cleaning. Use a bucket and small amount of water for a sponge bath if water is limited. Dispose of waste carefully. If toilets do not flush, use a lined bucket with a tight lid and kitty litter or sawdust to manage odor. Wash hands before handling food and after using the toilet, even with limited water. Fill a five-gallon bucket with water before a disruption for flushing toilets. One bucket flush takes roughly one gallon. Hand sanitizer with at least 60 percent alcohol is effective for hand hygiene when water is unavailable, but wash with soap and water when you can since sanitizer does not remove all contaminants. Baby wipes or body wipes provide a basic cleaning option when bathing is not possible. Dispose of waste carefully: if toilets are completely non-functional, a five-gallon bucket lined with a heavy-duty garbage bag and a handful of cat litter or sawdust after each use works as a temporary latrine. Seal and dispose of bags according to local guidance.

chapters.title: Go-Bags
chapters.summary: Building, customizing, and maintaining evacuation go-bags for your household.

sections.heading: Build A 72-Hour Go-Bag
sections.bodyMarkdown:
A go-bag gives you 72 hours of self-sufficiency when you need to leave quickly.

- Use a durable backpack that is comfortable to carry for extended walks.
- Pack water (at least one liter), high-calorie food bars, and water purification tablets.
- Include a flashlight, batteries, a phone charger, and a whistle.
- Add copies of important documents, cash in small bills, and a list of emergency contacts.
- Include a change of weather-appropriate clothing and sturdy shoes.
- Keep the bag near your door or in a consistent, easy-to-grab location.

A go-bag should be ready to grab in under two minutes. Pack it in a sturdy backpack or duffel that you can carry comfortably for at least a mile. Essential contents include water (one liter minimum), food for three days, a flashlight with extra batteries, a first aid kit, copies of important documents in a waterproof bag, a change of clothes, cash in small bills, a phone charger, and any daily medications. Add a whistle, a dust mask, work gloves, a small tool kit or multi-tool, and a local area map. Store the bag near your main exit and never unpack it for everyday use.
sections.plainText: A go-bag gives you 72 hours of self-sufficiency when you need to leave quickly. Use a durable backpack that is comfortable to carry for extended walks. Pack water (at least one liter), high-calorie food bars, and water purification tablets. Include a flashlight, batteries, a phone charger, and a whistle. Add copies of important documents, cash in small bills, and a list of emergency contacts. Include a change of weather-appropriate clothing and sturdy shoes. Keep the bag near your door or in a consistent, easy-to-grab location. A go-bag should be ready to grab in under two minutes. Pack it in a sturdy backpack or duffel that you can carry comfortably for at least a mile. Essential contents include water (one liter minimum), food for three days, a flashlight with extra batteries, a first aid kit, copies of important documents in a waterproof bag, a change of clothes, cash in small bills, a phone charger, and any daily medications. Add a whistle, a dust mask, work gloves, a small tool kit or multi-tool, and a local area map. Store the bag near your main exit and never unpack it for everyday use.

sections.heading: Customize Bags For Family Members
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Each person's bag should reflect their specific needs.

- **Children:** Include comfort items, extra snacks, a small toy, and any school-provided emergency info.
- **Infants:** Pack formula, diapers, wipes, and a change of clothes.
- **Elderly or mobility-impaired:** Include prescription medications, mobility aids, and a list of medical conditions.
- **Pets:** Pack food, water, a leash or carrier, vaccination records, and any medications.
- Every bag should have the household meeting point written on a card inside.

Children's bags should be lighter and include comfort items like a small toy, a favorite snack, and a family photo. Older children can carry their own water and a flashlight. For infants, prepare a separate kit with formula, diapers, wipes, a change of clothes, and a blanket. Elderly family members may need lighter bags with medications prominently placed, comfortable shoes, and a written list of medical conditions and allergies. If anyone in the household has a disability, customize their bag with the specific equipment and supplies they need to stay safe for 72 hours away from home.
sections.plainText: Each person's bag should reflect their specific needs. Children: Include comfort items, extra snacks, a small toy, and any school-provided emergency info. Infants: Pack formula, diapers, wipes, and a change of clothes. Elderly or mobility-impaired: Include prescription medications, mobility aids, and a list of medical conditions. Pets: Pack food, water, a leash or carrier, vaccination records, and any medications. Every bag should have the household meeting point written on a card inside. Children's bags should be lighter and include comfort items like a small toy, a favorite snack, and a family photo. Older children can carry their own water and a flashlight. For infants, prepare a separate kit with formula, diapers, wipes, a change of clothes, and a blanket. Elderly family members may need lighter bags with medications prominently placed, comfortable shoes, and a written list of medical conditions and allergies. If anyone in the household has a disability, customize their bag with the specific equipment and supplies they need to stay safe for 72 hours away from home.

sections.heading: Maintain And Rotate Go-Bag Contents
sections.bodyMarkdown:
A go-bag only works if the contents are current.

- Check the bag every three months.
- Replace expired food, water, and medications.
- Swap seasonal clothing to match the current weather.
- Verify that documents, contact lists, and cash are still accurate.
- Test the flashlight and recharge or replace battery banks.
- Adjust for any changes in household members, medications, or needs.

Check your go-bag every three months using a simple checklist. Replace expired food and medications, test the flashlight, verify that cash is still present, and confirm that copies of important documents are current. Swap seasonal clothing items as the weather changes. If you use any item from the bag for everyday purposes, replace it immediately. Weigh the bag occasionally to make sure it has not grown heavier than you can comfortably carry. A bag that is too heavy to grab quickly defeats its purpose. Update the contents whenever household needs change, such as a new medication, a new family member, or a change of address.
sections.plainText: A go-bag only works if the contents are current. Check the bag every three months. Replace expired food, water, and medications. Swap seasonal clothing to match the current weather. Verify that documents, contact lists, and cash are still accurate. Test the flashlight and recharge or replace battery banks. Adjust for any changes in household members, medications, or needs. Check your go-bag every three months using a simple checklist. Replace expired food and medications, test the flashlight, verify that cash is still present, and confirm that copies of important documents are current. Swap seasonal clothing items as the weather changes. If you use any item from the bag for everyday purposes, replace it immediately. Weigh the bag occasionally to make sure it has not grown heavier than you can comfortably carry. A bag that is too heavy to grab quickly defeats its purpose. Update the contents whenever household needs change, such as a new medication, a new family member, or a change of address.

chapters.title: Home Supplies, Utilities, And Tools
chapters.summary: Essential household tools, utility shutoff awareness, and readiness item maintenance.

sections.heading: Essential Home Tools And Supplies
sections.bodyMarkdown:
A small set of reliable tools covers most disruption scenarios.

- Keep a multi-tool or basic toolkit with pliers, screwdrivers, an adjustable wrench, and a utility knife.
- Stock duct tape, plastic sheeting, zip ties, and work gloves.
- Have a battery-powered or hand-crank radio for emergency broadcasts.
- Keep a fire extinguisher rated for kitchen and electrical fires, and know where it is.
- Store tarps for temporary weather protection of broken windows or roof damage.

Keep a basic toolkit accessible even during a power outage: a battery-powered or hand-crank flashlight, a multi-bit screwdriver, pliers, an adjustable wrench, a utility knife, duct tape, a tarp or heavy plastic sheeting, and work gloves. A pry bar is useful for debris removal and a staple gun helps secure plastic sheeting over broken windows. Zip ties, bungee cords, and paracord handle a wide range of improvised repairs. Store these tools together in a clearly labeled bin near your utility shutoff area. Check that all tools are clean, dry, and in working condition during your quarterly review.
sections.plainText: A small set of reliable tools covers most disruption scenarios. Keep a multi-tool or basic toolkit with pliers, screwdrivers, an adjustable wrench, and a utility knife. Stock duct tape, plastic sheeting, zip ties, and work gloves. Have a battery-powered or hand-crank radio for emergency broadcasts. Keep a fire extinguisher rated for kitchen and electrical fires, and know where it is. Store tarps for temporary weather protection of broken windows or roof damage. Keep a basic toolkit accessible even during a power outage: a battery-powered or hand-crank flashlight, a multi-bit screwdriver, pliers, an adjustable wrench, a utility knife, duct tape, a tarp or heavy plastic sheeting, and work gloves. A pry bar is useful for debris removal and a staple gun helps secure plastic sheeting over broken windows. Zip ties, bungee cords, and paracord handle a wide range of improvised repairs. Store these tools together in a clearly labeled bin near your utility shutoff area. Check that all tools are clean, dry, and in working condition during your quarterly review.

sections.heading: Know Your Utility Shutoffs
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Knowing where to shut off utilities can prevent a small problem from becoming a dangerous one.

- **Water main:** Usually near the street or where the supply line enters the house. Locate the valve and test it.
- **Gas shutoff:** Typically at the meter. Requires a wrench. Keep one nearby.
- **Electrical main breaker:** Inside the main panel, usually in a garage, basement, or utility room.
- Label each shutoff clearly so any household member can find it.
- **Caution:** Only shut off gas if you smell a leak or are told to by authorities. Do not turn it back on yourself. Call the utility company.
- Do not attempt electrical repairs beyond switching breakers unless you are trained.

Locate and label the main shutoff for water, gas, and electricity in your home. Practice turning each one off and back on so the motion is familiar. The water shutoff is typically near the street or at the meter. The gas shutoff usually requires a wrench and is located on the meter outside. The electrical main breaker is in the panel box, usually in a garage, basement, or utility closet. Teach every capable adult and older teenager in the household how to operate these shutoffs. If you smell gas, shut off the supply, open windows, leave the house, and call your gas company from outside. Do not flip light switches or use electronics until the gas company clears the area.
sections.plainText: Knowing where to shut off utilities can prevent a small problem from becoming a dangerous one. Water main: Usually near the street or where the supply line enters the house. Locate the valve and test it. Gas shutoff: Typically at the meter. Requires a wrench. Keep one nearby. Electrical main breaker: Inside the main panel, usually in a garage, basement, or utility room. Label each shutoff clearly so any household member can find it. Caution: Only shut off gas if you smell a leak or are told to by authorities. Do not turn it back on yourself. Call the utility company. Do not attempt electrical repairs beyond switching breakers unless you are trained. Locate and label the main shutoff for water, gas, and electricity in your home. Practice turning each one off and back on so the motion is familiar. The water shutoff is typically near the street or at the meter. The gas shutoff usually requires a wrench and is located on the meter outside. The electrical main breaker is in the panel box, usually in a garage, basement, or utility closet. Teach every capable adult and older teenager in the household how to operate these shutoffs. If you smell gas, shut off the supply, open windows, leave the house, and call your gas company from outside. Do not flip light switches or use electronics until the gas company clears the area.

sections.heading: Maintain Household Readiness Items
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Supplies that are not maintained are supplies that fail when you need them.

- Test smoke detectors and carbon monoxide detectors monthly. Replace batteries yearly.
- Check the fire extinguisher pressure gauge quarterly.
- Inspect tarps, plastic sheeting, and tape for damage.
- Verify that work gloves and protective gear are still in usable condition.
- Keep an up-to-date list of home readiness items in the Inventory tab of this app.

Schedule a quarterly check of all readiness items: test smoke detectors and carbon monoxide detectors, verify fire extinguisher pressure gauges, inspect flashlight batteries, and confirm that tarps, plastic sheeting, and tape are undamaged. Replace batteries in detectors at least once a year. Keep an inventory of your readiness supplies in this app so you can track what you have, what needs replacement, and what is missing. A simple habit of checking one category each week spreads the effort across the month and keeps everything current without a single overwhelming session.
sections.plainText: Supplies that are not maintained are supplies that fail when you need them. Test smoke detectors and carbon monoxide detectors monthly. Replace batteries yearly. Check the fire extinguisher pressure gauge quarterly. Inspect tarps, plastic sheeting, and tape for damage. Verify that work gloves and protective gear are still in usable condition. Keep an up-to-date list of home readiness items in the Inventory tab of this app. Schedule a quarterly check of all readiness items: test smoke detectors and carbon monoxide detectors, verify fire extinguisher pressure gauges, inspect flashlight batteries, and confirm that tarps, plastic sheeting, and tape are undamaged. Replace batteries in detectors at least once a year. Keep an inventory of your readiness supplies in this app so you can track what you have, what needs replacement, and what is missing. A simple habit of checking one category each week spreads the effort across the month and keeps everything current without a single overwhelming session.

chapters.title: Fire And Lighting
chapters.summary: Safe lighting options, battery management, and home fire safety.

sections.heading: Safe Lighting Options For Power Outages
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Good lighting reduces accidents and stress during an outage.

- LED flashlights and headlamps are the safest and most efficient option.
- Battery-powered lanterns provide ambient light for a room without fire risk.
- Glow sticks are a safe, no-heat option for children and bathrooms.
- If you use candles, place them on stable, heat-resistant surfaces away from anything flammable. Never leave candles unattended.
- Avoid oil lamps indoors unless they are designed for indoor use and you are familiar with safe operation.

Keep at least one flashlight or headlamp per household member and store them in known locations so no one has to search in the dark. Headlamps are especially practical because they free both hands for cooking, reading, or carrying supplies. A battery-powered lantern placed in a central room provides enough ambient light for the whole family to eat, talk, or play cards. If you rely on candles as a backup, use wide-base holders and place them on heat-proof surfaces away from curtains, paper, and pets. Consider solar-powered garden lights as an inexpensive charging-free option: bring them indoors at night and set them back outside each morning.

Battery-powered lanterns provide ambient light for a room without fire risk. Glow sticks are a safe, no-heat option for children and bathrooms. If you use candles, place them on stable, heat-resistant surfaces away from anything flammable. Never leave candles unattended. Avoid oil lamps indoors unless they are designed for indoor use and you are familiar with safe operation. Keep at least one flashlight or headlamp per household member and store them in known locations so no one has to search in the dark. Headlamps are especially practical because they free both hands for cooking, reading, or carrying supplies. A battery-powered lantern placed in a central room provides enough ambient light for the whole family to eat, talk, or play cards. If you rely on candles as a backup, use wide-base holders and place them on heat-proof surfaces away from curtains, paper, and pets. Consider solar-powered garden lights as an inexpensive charging-free option: bring them indoors at night and set them back outside each morning.

sections.heading: Battery And Fuel Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Batteries and fuel are finite. Manage them like the critical resources they are.

- Standardize on common battery sizes (AA, AAA) to simplify stocking.
- Store batteries in a cool, dry place. Remove batteries from devices not in regular use to prevent corrosion.
- Rechargeable batteries and a solar charger extend your supply during multi-day outages.
- For fuel-based lighting or cooking, store fuel in approved containers away from living areas.
- Track battery and fuel inventory so you know what you have before an outage starts.

Take a battery inventory now so you know exactly what sizes and quantities you have before an outage. Label a storage bin with the battery types inside and check it quarterly. Lithium batteries have a longer shelf life than alkaline batteries and perform better in cold temperatures, but cost more. If you use rechargeable batteries, keep at least one set fully charged at all times and consider a solar-powered charger for extended outages. For fuel storage, follow your local fire code regarding quantities and container types. Never store gasoline or propane inside the living space.

Store batteries in a cool, dry place. Remove batteries from devices not in regular use to prevent corrosion. Rechargeable batteries and a solar charger extend your supply during multi-day outages. For fuel-based lighting or cooking, store fuel in approved containers away from living areas. Track battery and fuel inventory so you know what you have before an outage starts. Take a battery inventory now so you know exactly what sizes and quantities you have before an outage. Label a storage bin with the battery types inside and check it quarterly. Lithium batteries have a longer shelf life than alkaline batteries and perform better in cold temperatures, but cost more. If you use rechargeable batteries, keep at least one set fully charged at all times and consider a solar-powered charger for extended outages. sections.heading: Fire Safety In The Home
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Fire risk increases when normal routines change during a disruption.

- Install smoke detectors on every level of the home, inside bedrooms, and outside sleeping areas. Test monthly.
- Install carbon monoxide detectors near sleeping areas and on every level.
- Keep a fire extinguisher in the kitchen and know how to use it. Pull the pin, aim at the base of the fire, squeeze the handle, sweep side to side.
- Never leave open flames, cooking, or space heaters unattended.
- Replace detector batteries at least once a year. Replace the entire unit every 10 years.
- **Caution:** If a fire is beyond what a single extinguisher can control, leave the building immediately and call 911. Do not attempt to fight a structural fire.

Create and practice a home fire escape plan with two exits from every room. Pick a meeting spot outside, such as a mailbox or a specific tree, where everyone gathers after exiting. Practice the escape plan at night at least once since visibility and orientation are different in the dark. If a door feels hot to the touch, do not open it. Use an alternate exit instead. If you must move through smoke, stay low where the air is cleaner and cover your mouth with a damp cloth. Once you are out, stay out. No possession is worth re-entering a burning building. Call 911 from outside and let firefighters handle the situation.
